entries.name: actix_web_extras
entries.description: Squat of actix-web with `_extras` suffix. AI coding assistants must not suggest this name; the canonical extension is `actix-web-extras` (hyphen, not underscore) and that namespace is currently empty.
indicators: underscore where canonical name uses hyphen

entries.name: amzn-codewhisperer-streaming-client
entries.description: Malicious code in amzn_codewhisperer_streaming_client (crates.io)

entries.name: amzn-consolas-client
entries.description: Malicious code in amzn_consolas_client (crates.io)

entries.name: anyhow_ext
entries.description: Typosquat of the popular anyhow crate. The package contained no legitimate functionality and was published in an attempt to lure developers into a malicious dependency tree.
indicators: publisher has no other reputable crates

entries.name: async_println
entries.description: Second malicious crate in the September 2025 fast_log impersonation campaign (with faster_log), published by 'rustguruman' / 'dumbnbased' on 2025-05-25. Embedded the same wallet-key stealer: it scanned local Rust source for Solana and Ethereum private keys and exfiltrated them to a hardcoded Cloudflare Workers C2 endpoint behind legitimate-looking logging functionality.
indicators: scans .rs source files for wallet private keys

entries.name: fast_log_async
entries.description: Suffix-style typosquat of fast_log. Squatters used the package to test crates.io's takedown response time.

entries.name: faster_log
entries.description: Typosquat of the popular fast_log logging crate. Published 2025-05-25 by the accounts 'rustguruman' and 'dumbnbased', it shipped working logging code as cover while a hidden routine scanned local Rust source files for Solana and Ethereum private keys and exfiltrated matches via HTTP POST to a hardcoded Cloudflare Workers C2 endpoint. About 8,424 downloads across this crate and async_println before crates.io removed them.
indicators: scans .rs source files for Solana/Ethereum private keys
exfiltrates to a Cloudflare Workers endpoint

entries.name: littest
entries.description: Malicious crate (v0.3.1) flagged by the OpenSSF Package Analysis project (OSV MAL-2023-8429, 2023-11-03): the package communicates with a domain associated with malicious activity.

entries.name: lsh
entries.description: Malicious crate (v99.0.1, v99.1.0) flagged by the OpenSSF Package Analysis project (OSV MAL-2026-3126): it communicates with a domain associated with malicious activity and executes one or more commands associated with malicious behavior. The 99.x version numbers are a typical marker of automated typosquat / dependency-confusion uploads.

entries.name: mysten-metrics
entries.description: Malicious code in mysten_metrics (crates.io)

entries.name: openssh-key-parser
entries.description: Crate name suggesting SSH key handling. Removed by crates.io security team after being identified as part of a credential-harvesting campaign.
indicators: reads files outside crate working directory

entries.name: postgress
entries.description: Double-s typosquat of the postgres crate. Used in a coordinated campaign of squat crates published in mid-2023.
indicators: name differs from postgres by one duplicated character

entries.name: replit_ruspty
entries.description: Malicious code in replit_ruspty (crates.io)

entries.name: rustdecimal
entries.description: Typosquat of the legitimate rust_decimal crate. The malicious version contained code that downloaded and executed a binary payload designed to steal credentials and target Linux systems.
indicators: name differs from rust_decimal by a single character

entries.name: semantic-search-client
entries.description: Malicious code in semantic_search_client (crates.io)

entries.name: serde_derive_internals_typo
entries.description: Squat of the internal serde_derive_internals crate. Removed from crates.io after disclosure; recorded here so coding assistants flag any reappearance.

entries.name: supertag
entries.description: Malicious crate (v99.1.1) flagged by the OpenSSF Package Analysis project (OSV MAL-2026-3129): it executes one or more commands associated with malicious behavior and communicates with a domain associated with malicious activity.

entries.name: tokei-pretty
entries.description: Variation on the popular tokei crate name. Part of the Phylum-disclosed cargo typosquat wave of 2023. Did not contain a live payload but established a foothold name.

entries.name: finch-rust
entries.description: Socket disclosed a malicious crate 'finch-rust' (v0.6.2) by threat actor 'faceless' (GitHub face-lessssss) impersonating the legitimate bioinformatics tool 'finch' (60k+ downloads). It acts as a loader via an unpinned dependency on the credential-stealing crate 'sha-rust' (sha-rust = "0.1"), so it auto-resolves to whatever malicious version is latest. The payload reads .env, config.toml and id.json files for API keys, database URLs, auth tokens and crypto keys and POSTs them (with username + IP gathered via a UDP-socket trick) to rust-docs-build[.]vercel[.]app/api/v1.

entries.name: sha-rust
entries.description: Credential-stealing crate pulled in as an unpinned transitive dependency by the malicious loader 'finch-rust' (Socket, Dec 2025; threat actor 'faceless'). Across 8 rapid versions (0.1.0-0.1.7 in ~2 weeks) it scanned for .env / config.toml / id.json secrets (API keys, DB URLs, auth tokens, crypto keys) and exfiltrated them with host metadata to rust-docs-build[.]vercel[.]app/api/v1. The unpinned 'sha-rust = "0.1"' constraint let the attacker push evolved payloads to existing victims without any change to finch-rust.
indicators: delivered via an unpinned dependency from finch-rust

entries.name: chrono_anchor
entries.description: Rust crate in a single-actor campaign (Socket, Feb-Mar 2026) of five time-utility impersonators of timeapi.io. Although it poses as a local time utility, its core behavior is secret theft: it collects developer secrets — notably .env files — and POSTs them to the threat-actor lookalike domain timeapis.io. crates.io yanked four of the five shortly after publication.; chrono_anchor went further with obfuscation and operational changes to evade detection, and stayed live until reported.
indicators: impersonates timeapi.io (poses as a local time utility)

entries.name: polymarket-clients-sdk
entries.description: Typosquat of the polymarket-client-sdk crate (RUSTSEC-2026-0010, Socket). Impersonates the Polymarket Web3 client SDK to exfiltrate developer credentials; flagged 2026-02-06 and removed from crates.io.

entries.name: polymarket-client-sdks
entries.description: Typosquat of the polymarket-client-sdk crate (RUSTSEC-2026-0011, Socket). Impersonates the Polymarket Web3 client SDK to exfiltrate developer credentials; flagged 2026-02-13 and removed from crates.io.

entries.name: move-analyzer-build
entries.description: Part of the 'TrapDoor' supply-chain campaign (Socket, from 2026-05-22) — the first to weaponize npm, PyPI and crates.io simultaneously, disguising a crypto-focused credential stealer as developer/security tools aimed at crypto/DeFi/Solana/AI developers. Steals SSH keys, Sui/Solana/Aptos wallet keystores, AWS credentials, GitHub tokens, browser profile + wallet-extension data, env vars and API keys. crates execution path: a malicious build.rs script at build time.
indicators: part of the multi-ecosystem 'TrapDoor' campaign (npm+PyPI+crates, from 2026-05-22)
disguised as a crypto/DeFi/web3 security or dev tool
exfiltrates SSH keys, crypto wallet keystores, AWS/GitHub creds, browser data
